text: Two Pointers Pattern: Two sum in sorted array. Use left=0, right=len-1 pointers. If sum < target: left++. If sum > target: right--. If sum == target: found. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Requires sorted input. Contrast with hash map O(n) for unsorted. Use when: input is sorted, or sorting is acceptable, looking for pair with given sum/difference.

Two Pointers Pattern: Remove duplicates from sorted array in-place. Slow pointer j tracks write position. Fast pointer i scans. If arr[i] != arr[j]: j++; arr[j]=arr[i]. Return j+1. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Variation: allow at most k duplicates — check arr[i] != arr[j-k] before including. Standard interview pattern.

Two Pointers Pattern: Merge two sorted arrays. Two pointers from start of each array. Compare and take smaller element. Advance that array's pointer. Continue until one is exhausted, then append rest. Time: O(m+n), Space: O(m+n) for new array or O(1) if merging in-place from the end (merge into larger array from right). Foundation of merge sort.

Two Pointers Pattern: Palindrome check. left=0, right=len-1. While left<right: if s[left]!=s[right]: not palindrome. Advance both inward. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Variation: valid palindrome II — allow one deletion. Try skipping left or right character, check if remaining is palindrome. Time: O(n).

Two Pointers Pattern: Container with most water. Find two lines forming container with maximum water. Width = right-left, height = min(height[left], height[right]). Move pointer pointing to shorter line inward (can't do better by moving taller line). Time: O(n). Greedy proof: moving shorter pointer is the only way to potentially increase water volume.

Two Pointers Pattern: Trapping rain water. Two pointers from both ends. Track left_max and right_max. Water at current position = min(left_max, right_max) - height[current]. Process side with smaller max (it's the limiting factor). Advance that pointer. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Alternative: monotonic stack for O(n) with column-by-column computation.

Two Pointers Pattern: 3Sum (triplets summing to zero). Sort array. Fix one element with outer loop. Use two pointers on remaining sorted subarray for two-sum. Skip duplicates to avoid duplicate triplets. Time: O(n²), Space: O(1) excluding output. Generalizes to 4Sum with two nested loops + two pointers. Skip duplicate fixes: after match, advance both pointers past duplicates.

Two Pointers Pattern: Minimum window with constraint. Expand right pointer to satisfy constraint. Once satisfied, shrink from left to minimize window. Use for: minimum window substring, smallest subarray with sum >= target. Template: right expands to include, left contracts while constraint holds. Track best window. Time: O(n).

Two Pointers Pattern: Squares of sorted array. Input is sorted with negatives. Two pointers from both ends. Larger absolute value is at either end. Compare abs(left) and abs(right), place larger square at end of result, move that pointer inward. Time: O(n). More efficient than squaring then sorting O(n log n).

Two Pointers Pattern: Partition (QuickSort / Dutch flag). Rearrange array around pivot. Two pointers: write_pos and read_pos. If arr[read] <= pivot: swap with write_pos, advance both. Else: advance read only. For three-way partition (Dutch flag): three pointers lo, mid, hi. Time: O(n) per partition.

Two Pointers Pattern: Find all pairs with difference K. Sort array. Pointers left=0, right=1. If diff==k: record, advance both. If diff<k: advance right. If diff>k: advance left. Skip duplicates. Time: O(n log n) with sort. Alternative: for each element x, binary search for x+k. Time: O(n log n). Hash set: O(n) time.

Two Pointers Pattern: Move zeros to end. Write pointer for non-zero elements. Scan with read pointer. When non-zero found: swap with write position, advance write. All non-zeros moved forward, zeros fill back. Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Variation: move all instances of value v to end. Same template — write pointer skips over v.

Two Pointers Pattern: Linked list cycle detection (Floyd's). Slow pointer advances 1, fast pointer advances 2. If they meet, cycle exists. To find cycle start: reset slow to head, advance both by 1 step at a time until they meet — meeting point is cycle start. Proof based on mathematical relationship between distances. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

Two Pointers Pattern: Find middle of linked list. Slow advances 1, fast advances 2. When fast reaches end, slow is at middle. For even-length list: slow lands on first or second middle depending on initialization. Use for: merge sort on linked list, palindrome linked list check (reverse second half). Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

Two Pointers Pattern: Longest substring without repeating characters. Sliding window with two pointers. Expand right, track characters in window with hash map. When duplicate found: shrink from left until no duplicate. Track max window size. Time: O(n), Space: O(min(n, charset)). This is also a sliding window pattern; two-pointer and sliding window often overlap.

Two Pointers Pattern: Intersection of two sorted arrays. Two pointers advance through both arrays simultaneously. If equal: record intersection, advance both. If arr1[i] < arr2[j]: advance i. Else: advance j. Time: O(m+n). For sorted arrays this beats hash set O(m+n) by using O(1) space (if duplicates handled carefully).

Two Pointers Pattern: Subarrays with product less than K. Count subarrays where product < k. Right pointer expands window. If product >= k, shrink from left. Number of valid subarrays ending at right = right - left + 1. Accumulate. Time: O(n). Works because all subarrays within window are valid — subarrays ending at right with any start in [left, right].

Two Pointers Pattern: Reverse words or array segments. In-place reverse of entire array: two pointers from ends, swap and move inward. Reverse words in string: reverse entire string, then reverse each word. Or tokenize words, reverse list, rejoin. Two-pointer reversal is O(n), Space: O(1) for in-place array reversal.

Two Pointers Pattern: Sort colors / Dutch national flag. Classic two-pointer + one extra pointer. lo=0 (boundary for 0s), mid=0 (scan pointer), hi=n-1 (boundary for 2s). If arr[mid]==0: swap(lo,mid), lo++, mid++. If arr[mid]==1: mid++. If arr[mid]==2: swap(mid,hi), hi-- (don't advance mid). Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Single pass.

Two Pointers Pattern: When to use two pointers vs sliding window vs binary search. Two pointers: fixed two endpoints converging toward each other (sorted array problems, pair finding). Sliding window: fixed or variable window moving in one direction (substring problems, subarray sum). Binary search: when answer is monotonic (optimization, search space). Overlap: many sliding window problems use two pointer mechanics.
